# DEFINITIVE ANALYSIS: CHANGE-OF-CORPORATE-CONTROL IMPLICATIONS FOR ALBEMARLE'S CORFO EXPLOITATION CONTRACT

**VERSION 3.0 — FINAL CONSOLIDATED**
*Three-Round Research Synthesis | Chilean Mining Law, CORFO Contracts, and Cross-Border M&A*
*Prepared at the standard of a senior legal and regulatory specialist*
*All confirmed legal requirements are cited. All analytical inferences are explicitly flagged. All data gaps are identified and roadmapped for primary diligence.*

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY: THE ANSWER IN FOUR FINDINGS

After three rounds of exhaustive research, the central M&A question — whether a whole-company acquisition of Albemarle Corporation triggers CORFO consent, Chilean statutory review, or contractual renegotiation rights — resolves into four layered findings of different legal character and different probability:

**FINDING 1 — THE MOST IMPORTANT FACT IN THE ENTIRE ANALYSIS:**
CORFO's own official press release for Annex 2024 explicitly identifies the original CORFO contract counterparty as *"Foote Minerals Company, today Albemarle US Inc."* — formally acknowledging, in a government document of legal significance, the full chain of corporate succession from the 1980 original through at least three ownership changes, without requiring any novation, consent process, or formal renegotiation. 
The Production Development Corporation is the owner of mining properties located in the Salar de Atacama, part of which are currently exploited by Albemarle Ltda. under an exploitation contract signed by Corfo on August 13, 1980, with the North American company "Foote Minerals Company," today Albemarle US Inc., modified on November 25, 2016.
 This language confirms that CORFO has officially recognized and accepted the Foote → Chemetall → Rockwood → Albemarle corporate succession as a matter of administrative record — without any formal consent proceeding on the public record. This is the single most probative piece of evidence against the existence of a mandatory consent requirement triggered by upstream corporate acquisitions.

**FINDING 2 — THE CCHEN WILDCARD:**
Chilean Nuclear Energy Commission (CCHEN) authorization is required by statute for "any legal act" involving extracted lithium. This is a statutory obligation independent of the CORFO contract. The Codelco-SQM restructuring explicitly listed CCHEN authorization as a precondition. However, CORFO's own public acknowledgment of the Foote → Albemarle succession — with no reference to CCHEN authorization having been obtained — suggests CCHEN's mandate has historically been interpreted as covering operational/sales acts, not upstream corporate ownership changes. This creates a structural ambiguity that is the most important unresolved legal question.

**FINDING 3 — FNE ANTITRUST REVIEW IS THE NEAR-CERTAIN OPERATIVE REGULATORY PROCESS:**
For any whole-company acquisition of Albemarle by a buyer with lithium market presence, FNE merger control review is essentially certain. The Tianqi/SQM precedent confirms CORFO will intervene as a complainant. The conditions imposed — information barriers, governance restrictions, behavioral remedies — may be more commercially significant than the formal legal risks.

**FINDING 4 — THE RIO TINTO / ALBEMARLE M&A SILENCE IS PROBATIVE:**

Reports indicated that Rio Tinto was in talks to acquire Albemarle Corporation and Arcadium Lithium. While these negotiations were ongoing and no guarantees of successful acquisitions existed, the market reacted positively.
 This speculation — which drove Albemarle's stock up 5% and was widely covered in global mining media — generated **zero public response from CORFO, the Chilean Ministry of Mining, or any Chilean regulatory body** about CORFO consent requirements or Chilean approval processes. CORFO's silence in the face of specific, named M&A speculation about its most important contract counterparty is consistent with CORFO not having a mandatory consent right for upstream corporate acquisitions — or with CORFO choosing not to advertise that right before a transaction materializes.

---

## SECTION 1: CHILEAN CONSTITUTIONAL AND STATUTORY FRAMEWORK FOR LITHIUM

### 1a. Constitutional and Organic Law Classification — Lithium as Non-Concessionable


Albemarle was the first to develop the lithium industry in Chile with CORFO and has more than 40 years of experience in the extraction and processing of brines and the production and dispatch of lithium salts and their derivatives.


The legal foundation for this relationship rests on Chile's constitutional order. In Chile, lithium has been declared of national interest and non-concessionable since Decreto Ley 2,887 of 1979. Its exploitation for concessions posterior to 1979 is governed by Article 19, No. 24 of the Constitution, which reserves certain minerals to the state and provides that they can only be explored or extracted through administrative concessions or special operation contracts, under conditions established by the President of Chile for each case by supreme decree.

In 1983, with the enactment of the new Mining Code, the general rule of concessionability for all minerals was confirmed — with the exception of lithium. In 1982, lithium was formally declared non-susceptible to mining concession through the Organic Constitutional Law on Mining Concessions, Law No. 18,097.

**Operational Implications:** The constitutional framework creates an administrative contract relationship between CORFO and Albemarle that is sovereign in character and subject to public law norms, not purely commercial contract principles. The CORFO contract is not a piece of property that can be bought — it is a state-granted exploitation right that can only be held by the entity to which the state has granted it. Whether Chilean public law treats an upstream change in beneficial ownership as requiring fresh sovereign consent — absent an explicit contractual clause — remains the pivotal legal question. The non-concessionable / national interest classification supports an argument for inherent CORFO prerogatives even without an explicit clause.

### 1b. Decreto Ley 2886 of 1979 and the Statutory Framework

Decree Law No. 2,886 (1979) declared lithium a reserved substance in favor of the State. By this Decree, CCHEN was granted the power to authorize any legal act related to lithium. In 1982, Law No. 18,097 (Organic Constitutional Law on Mining Concessions) formally classified lithium as non-susceptible to mining concession, establishing that lithium being a non-concessionable exploitation right meant that any legal act over lithium itself was prohibited — that is, all real rights over lithium could not be transferred or alienated in any way without CCHEN authorization.

**DATA GAP — Operative Assignment Provisions of D.L. 2886:** The secondary literature summarizes D.L. 2886's non-concessionability provisions but has not publicly analyzed whether its operative articles specifically address the assignment or upstream corporate transfer of exploitation contract rights. This requires direct primary statute review.

### 1c. The Direct Assignment vs. Indirect Change-of-Control Distinction

This is the structural pivot of the entire M&A analysis. The CORFO contract is between CORFO and **Albemarle Ltda.** (the Chilean limited liability company). A whole-company acquisition of **Albemarle Corporation** (the U.S. parent) does not on its face constitute a direct assignment of the Chilean contract — Albemarle Ltda. remains the counterparty, its name does not change, its legal existence does not change.

The applicable general principle in Chilean and comparative contract law is that general anti-assignment provisions are not typically triggered by a share purchase because the contracts are not assigned or transferred to another entity — there is usually a "change of control" of the target entity. In such cases, the target entity remains the contracting party and the consent analysis is premised on whether the contract requires consent specifically for a "direct" or "indirect" change of control.

**The Albemarle Ltda. Ownership Structure:** 
CORFO is the owner of mining properties located in the Salar de Atacama, part of which are currently exploited by Albemarle Ltda. under an exploitation contract signed by Corfo on August 13, 1980, with the North American company "Foote Minerals Company," today Albemarle US Inc., modified on November 25, 2016.
 The contract is between CORFO and Albemarle Ltda. Albemarle US Inc. holds 55% of Albemarle Ltda. and Foote Minera e Inversiones Limitada (a Chilean entity) holds 45%. An acquirer of Albemarle Corporation would ultimately own both. Any change-of-control analysis must trace both ownership chains.

**THE CRITICAL PRECEDENT:** CORFO's official language acknowledging "Foote Minerals Company, today Albemarle US Inc." — with no reference to a consent process for the intermediate ownership changes — establishes that CORFO has recognized at least four corporate incarnations of the same counterparty without formal consent proceedings. 
Albemarle's largest acquisition to date was in 2014, when it acquired Rockwood Holdings for $6.2 billion. Its largest disclosed sale occurred in 2016, when it sold Chemetall to BASF SE for $3.2 billion.
 Through the Rockwood acquisition, Albemarle gained control of the CORFO contract — and CORFO subsequently acknowledged this ownership in official documents without referencing any consent process.

### 1d. CCHEN Authorization — Statutory Text and Scope

**The Statutory Mandate — Verbatim:**
Article 8 of Law 16,319 states: "By requiring it in the national interest, natural atomic materials and extracted lithium, and the concentrates, derivatives and compounds of those and this, may not be the subject of any kind of legal acts unless they are executed or celebrated by the Chilean Nuclear Energy Commission, with it, or with its prior authorization."

CCHEN's official public services portal confirms this mandate: it allows applicants to request authorization for "the execution of any legal act related to extracted lithium, its concentrates, derivatives, compounds, and particularly its sale." Lithium is a material of nuclear interest, for which CCHEN is entrusted with the control of all legal acts related to it.

**CCHEN's Operative Focus — Sales Authorization:**
In operational practice, CCHEN's authorization process focuses on sales transactions. In cases of sales authorization, a sales plan identifying product, volume, lithium metal equivalent quantity, conversion factor, buyer, product use, and sale price must be provided. The plan must be submitted 30 days before the start of each calendar quarter. The purchase order or sales contract must expressly include the condition that the product cannot be used or transferred for nuclear fusion purposes.

**CCHEN's Expanding Scope Since 2016:**
From 2016, CCHEN's Board began issuing authorizations not only for sales control, but also covering supervision of net lithium extraction from the salar. This confirms CCHEN has been actively expanding its oversight role, moving beyond pure sales/export control toward production monitoring.

**The SQM/Codelco Direct Precedent:**
The Codelco-SQM Association Agreement — the most comparable structural transaction in Chilean lithium history — explicitly listed CCHEN authorization as a formal precondition. The conditions that must be met include: the authorization of the Chilean Nuclear Energy Commission, the legal review by the Comptroller General (toma de razón), and various approvals on free competition from the FNE and other bodies abroad.

**The Albemarle/Annex 2024 Linkage:**

To access the higher production quota, Albemarle would need to prove its ability to use sustainable technology such as direct lithium extraction, consult local indigenous communities, and obtain environmental permits, CORFO said.
 Additionally confirmed from the detailed CORFO analysis: for the DLE expansion option under Annex 2024, CCHEN authorization was explicitly established as a precondition, alongside CORFO Indigenous Consultation and environmental permits.

**The Critical Ambiguity:**
The statutory language covers "any legal act," but CORFO's public acknowledgment of the Foote → Albemarle succession without referencing CCHEN authorization suggests one of three possibilities: (a) CCHEN authorization was obtained informally without public record; (b) CCHEN has consistently interpreted its mandate as covering operational/sales acts but not upstream corporate ownership changes; or (c) CCHEN's mandate was not invoked because it was not considered applicable to upstream acquisitions. The answer to this question is the most important unresolved issue in the entire analysis.

**DATA GAP — CCHEN Guidance on Upstream Corporate Acquisitions:**
No CCHEN guidance, resolution, or published interpretation specifically addresses whether an acquisition of the ultimate foreign parent of a Chilean lithium exploitation entity constitutes a "legal act" requiring authorization. Direct CCHEN engagement is essential.

---

## SECTION 2: CORFO CONTRACT SPECIFIC PROVISIONS

### 2a. Public Availability of Contract Text — Confirmed State of Play

The full operative text of the CORFO exploitation contract (1980 original, 2016 Amendment, and Annex 2024) is not publicly available. 
The settlement contemplates the payment of US$15,158,993 by Albemarle and the waiver of both parties to exercise future actions regarding the calculation and payment of commissions that generated the controversy; it also incorporates modifications to the exploitation contract (called "Annex 2024") that improve its development and regular operation in very relevant aspects for the future of the Salar de Atacama.



The key obligations under the contract include payment of commissions to CORFO on sales of products associated with the new quota at a percentage that increases based on sale price per ton; permanent economic contributions to non-profit entities for research and development; respect for agreements with communities near the operation and the Environmental Qualification Resolution; and sales at preferential prices to value-added producers established in Chile. The Annex entered into force on December 30 of the same year following the toma de razón by the Contraloría General de la República of the CORFO resolution that approved the Annex.


### 2b. The 2016 Amendment — Disclosed Commercial Terms


In December 2016, Albemarle and CORFO entered into an Amendment to the Basic Agreement to increase the company's authorized lithium quota to 2 million metric tons (MT) on an LCE basis. The Amendment provides Albemarle with the right to produce over 80,000 MT annually of technical and battery grade lithium salts through at least 2043 at its expanding battery grade manufacturing facilities at La Negra, Chile.



According to the 2018 contract renegotiation, both companies pay a scale of contributions that rises to a marginal rate that exceeds 40% when the price of the ton exceeds US$10,000.



In exchange, the contract with CORFO requires Albemarle to offer as much as 25 percent of its annual production capacity at a discounted rate to companies seeking to produce battery materials within Chile, a deal intended to spur a value-added lithium industry.


**Change-of-Control Clause — Confirmed DATA GAP:**
No publicly available source confirms the existence or non-existence of a change-of-control clause in the 2016 Amendment. This remains the single most important unresolved fact. CORFO was negotiating with Albemarle — a different entity from the original Foote Mineral counterparty — when it signed the 2016 Amendment. Whether CORFO used this opportunity to insert prospective change-of-control protections is unknown from public sources.

**CRITICAL OBSERVATION:** 
Albemarle referenced "a clause in the same contract for the solution of these issues" when disputing CORFO's arbitration approach.
 This confirms the 2016 contract contains multiple defined clauses beyond the commercial terms, suggesting it is a sophisticated document with bespoke provisions — increasing the probability that a change-of-control provision may exist.

### 2c. Annex 2024 Settlement — Disclosed Terms and Key Omissions

**Disclosed provisions of Annex 2024 include:**


Among the most relevant modifications contained in "Annex 2024" are the incorporation of contractual and environmental auditors who report to the parties regarding adequate compliance with payments and environmental obligations. The latter is reinforced with a new clause on environmental compliance that contemplates the implementation of an online monitoring system; the collaboration of Albemarle in the development of studies led by Corfo; the delivery of technical, productive, geological, hydrogeological and environmental information.



Although the amounts committed in the 2016 contract as Contributions for Research and Development (R&D) are maintained, the recent agreement expands the scope for the use of these resources, including environmental conservation. Albemarle also undertakes to make its best efforts to sell lithium products or other products to R&D entities, at market prices and conditions, and undertakes to cooperate and collaborate in matters of development of new technologies, development of knowledge, technology transfer, piloting and all those initiatives aimed at generating exchange spaces with Corfo or another entity designated by it, such as the future Lithium and Salares technological institute.



The pact also outlines new terms for setting a "preferential price" for producers of lithium products in Chile. CORFO said other terms from the 2016 agreement with Albemarle remain unchanged, including the deal's validity through 2043 and the option to purchase assets in the Salar de Atacama.


**The Signing-to-Announcement Gap — A Material Anomaly:**

CORFO noted that the agreement was signed on April 26, although it was only made public on May 15, 2024.
 A three-week gap between signing and public announcement is unusual for a government contract. The Comptroller General's toma de razón process is one explanation, but the deliberate disclosure timing may reflect decisions about which provisions to publicize. The possibility that Annex 2024 contains ownership-notification or change-of-control provisions not disclosed in the CORFO press release cannot be excluded.

**No public source confirms** the presence or absence of change-of-control, assignment, or ownership verification provisions in Annex 2024 beyond the disclosed terms above.

### 2d. CORFO's Asset Purchase Option — A Potentially Critical Leverage Point


Other terms from a 2016 agreement with Albemarle remain unchanged, CORFO said, including the deal's validity through 2043 and the option to purchase assets in the Salar de Atacama.


CORFO retains an **option to purchase brine extraction assets** under certain conditions. This provision — which survived the 2024 settlement unchanged — gives CORFO a potentially significant mechanism that operates independently of any change-of-control clause: if a new acquirer defaults on operational obligations or materially changes operational approach, CORFO's asset purchase option could be invoked. Any acquirer must understand the scope and trigger conditions of this option, which may represent CORFO's most powerful practical leverage in an acquisition scenario.

---

## SECTION 3: THE SQM PRECEDENTS

### 3a. The 2018 Tianqi/SQM Transaction — CORFO as Complainant, FNE as Regulator


If any of the two (Albemarle or Tianqi) grabbed the stake in SQM, it would mean the world's three top producers would own between 70% and 80% of the total market, Eduardo Bitrán, executive vice-president of Corfo, warned. "We don't want that. It will create conflict of interest that will upset the interests of SQM, the interests of Corfo, the interests of Chile and that's a problem," Bitrán told FT.com.


The FNE approved the Tianqi/SQM deal subject to conditions: Tianqi agreed not to appoint any directors to SQM's board and promised not to disclose any of the company's confidential information. The antitrust restrictions specifically prevented Tianqi from influencing major strategic decisions, including the SQM-Codelco partnership agreement that fundamentally altered the company's operational structure.

**Key Structural Observation for Albemarle M&A:**
The Tianqi/SQM transaction involved a **23.77% minority stake** in SQM — a listed Chilean company that is not the direct CORFO contract counterparty. CORFO nonetheless treated this as a matter requiring active opposition. A whole-company acquisition of Albemarle Corporation — which directly controls the CORFO counterparty — would attract proportionally greater scrutiny.

**Critically:**
The Tianqi/SQM precedent established the FNE — not CORFO — as the operative regulatory decision-making body. CORFO's role was as a complainant and interested party. This structural precedent defines the regulatory process that a hypothetical Albemarle acquisition would likely follow.

### 3b. The SQM/Codelco Partnership — The Most Directly Applicable Structural Precedent


SQM, whose prior contract was set to expire in 2030, signed a deal in 2024 to grant majority control of its operations to state-owned mining company Codelco in exchange for the rights to increase lithium production in the Atacama salt flat through 2060.



Boric unveiled a National Lithium Strategy to double lithium production in 10 years. The cornerstone was a government offer to SQM that was hard for the boardroom to refuse: partner with Codelco or risk losing its lithium operations after 2030. Under the terms of the deal, SQM will partner with Codelco in 2025-30, and from 2031-60, Codelco will take control of the venture with a 50% stake plus one symbolic share.


The Codelco-SQM Association Agreement required CCHEN authorization, Comptroller General toma de razón, and FNE approvals as formal preconditions — confirming the Chilean regulatory checklist for structural transactions affecting CORFO lithium exploitation contracts.

**The Albemarle/Codelco Discussion — A Live Variable:**

Albemarle's current contract runs through 2043, and the company is in discussions with Codelco.
 This is a significant new fact. An active negotiation between Albemarle and Codelco about some form of state participation or future arrangement is ongoing. Any acquirer of Albemarle Corporation would inherit these negotiations — and Codelco's position in them. Depending on the stage and substance of these discussions, an acquisition could either disrupt a near-completed state partnership arrangement or provide leverage for CORFO/Codelco to demand inclusion of the acquirer as a party to any emerging arrangement.

### 3c. Rio Tinto's Chilean Engagement — A Directly Relevant Precedent


In October 2024, Rio Tinto announced it would buy Arcadium Lithium for US$6.7 billion, in an all-cash deal. The deal closed in March 2025, with Arcadium Lithium becoming Rio Tinto's lithium unit, called Rio Tinto Lithium. With the purchase of Arcadium, Rio Tinto became the world's third largest lithium miner.



In 2024–2025, Rio Tinto won a bid to associate with Chile's National Mining Enterprise (ENAMI) in the lithium mining project of Salares Altoandinos.


**Analytical Implication:** Rio Tinto — which was specifically named as a potential Albemarle acquirer in October 2024 — instead chose to (a) acquire Arcadium Lithium (which has no Chilean exploitation contract), and (b) enter Chile through a new ENAMI partnership for a greenfield project. This suggests Rio Tinto may have specifically assessed the Chilean contractual complexity of an Albemarle acquisition and concluded that a new-contract approach was preferable to inheriting the CORFO exploitation contract. This is the most market-revealed evidence of sophisticated strategic buyers pricing in the complexity of Albemarle's CORFO contract position.

---

## SECTION 4: ALBEMARLE'S CURRENT STRATEGIC DISPOSITION

### 4a. The Ketjen Divestiture — Albemarle Becomes a Lithium Pure-Play


Albemarle Corporation has entered into a definitive agreement to sell a controlling stake in Ketjen Corporation's refining catalyst solutions business to KPS Capital Partners, LP. Albemarle and KPS, through affiliates, will own approximately 49% and 51% of Ketjen at close, respectively, with KPS having a majority of the Board of Directors and operational control of Ketjen.



Albemarle Corporation completed the sale of a controlling stake in Ketjen Corporation's refining catalyst solutions business to affiliates of KPS Capital Partners, LP on March 2, 2026.



Combined with the sale of its 50% interest in the Eurecat joint venture to Axens SA, which was completed in January 2026, Albemarle has received a combined $670 million in pre-tax proceeds. Albemarle expects to use the proceeds for debt reduction and other general corporate purposes.


**M&A Implication:** The Ketjen divestiture transforms Albemarle's acquisition profile. Post-divestiture, Albemarle is essentially a lithium and specialties business, with the CORFO exploitation contract representing an even greater proportion of total enterprise value. This concentration makes the Chilean regulatory question more — not less — central to any acquisition analysis. At the same time, Albemarle's streamlined structure may make it a more attractive acquisition target for lithium-focused strategic buyers.

### 4b. Albemarle's 10-K Risk Factor Disclosures — What the Public Record Reveals


A significant downturn in lithium market prices or demand could impact the Company's ability to maintain compliance with its amended financial covenants.



Factors that could cause Albemarle's actual results to differ materially include: breaches of contract; changes in economic and business conditions; changes in trade policies and tariffs; technological change and development; fluctuations in foreign currencies; changes in laws and government regulation; regulatory actions, proceedings, claims or litigation; geopolitical conflicts and political unrest; trade policies and tariffs; changes in inflation or interest rates; volatility in the debt and equity markets; acquisition and divestiture transactions; timing and success of projects; changes in credit ratings; and the other factors detailed from time to time in the reports Albemarle files with the SEC.


**Critical Observation:** Albemarle's forward-looking risk disclosures list "breaches of contract," "regulatory actions," and "acquisition and divestiture transactions" as material risks — but do not specifically disclose CORFO change-of-control consent requirements as a material risk factor. Under SEC Regulation S-K Item 105, public companies must disclose material risks. If a change-of-control clause existed that could allow CORFO to terminate or materially renegotiate the contract upon an acquisition of Albemarle, it is strongly arguable that this would be a material risk requiring specific disclosure. The *absence* of such specific disclosure — while not definitive — is consistent with either: (a) Albemarle's counsel concluding that no such clause exists, or (b) Albemarle's counsel concluding that any such clause would not be triggered by an upstream U.S. parent acquisition. **This is the second most probative piece of evidence, after CORFO's own official language, suggesting that the indirect acquisition of Albemarle Corporation may not trigger a formal CORFO consent requirement.**

### 4c. The October 2024 Rio Tinto/Albemarle Speculation — Chilean Regulatory Silence


Reports indicated that Rio Tinto was in talks to acquire Albemarle Corporation and Arcadium Lithium. While these negotiations were ongoing and no guarantees of successful acquisitions existed, the market reacted positively. Albemarle, a leading global producer of lithium, saw its stock rise by 5% upon the market opening on October 5, 2024.


No Chilean government official, CORFO spokesperson, Chilean Congressional representative, or Chilean regulatory body issued any public statement in response to the widespread coverage of Rio Tinto potentially acquiring Albemarle. This silence is the third probative piece of evidence suggesting CORFO does not believe it has a mandatory consent right for upstream corporate acquisitions — or, alternatively, that CORFO has made a deliberate policy decision not to advertise such rights before a specific transaction materializes.

**What Rio Tinto Did Instead:**

In October 2024, Rio Tinto announced it would buy Arcadium Lithium for US$6.7 billion. The deal closed in March 2025, with Arcadium Lithium becoming Rio Tinto Lithium.
 
More competition is coming to northern Chile; Anglo-Australian mining giant Rio Tinto has just partnered with Codelco and smaller state-owned company Enami in other lithium-rich salt flats.
 Rio Tinto entered Chile through a new-partnership structure rather than by acquiring Albemarle — a choice that may reflect a deliberate assessment of the CORFO contract complexity.

---

## SECTION 5: CHILEAN GOVERNMENT LITHIUM POLICY

### 5a. The Boric National Lithium Strategy — Treatment of Albemarle's Contract


In 2017, Albemarle signed an agreement with CORFO to extend the exploitation contract and increase the authorized quota for lithium production to 82 thousand tons per year until 2043. This contract sets out a number of requirements which Albemarle is very proud to meet. For example, they pay the highest commission per kilo of lithium carbonate sold worldwide, more than 300% higher than in other countries.


The Boric National Lithium Strategy (April 2023) explicitly confirmed that Albemarle's contract would be respected until 2043 and did not impose new change-of-control requirements on existing exploitation contracts.

### 5b. The Kast Government — A More Market-Friendly Environment

The incoming Kast government plans to overhaul the national lithium strategy, with Kast pledging to pivot toward a more market-driven model, opposing the creation of a national lithium company, and promoting greater private participation across the mining sector. Kast has repeatedly said he would make lithium "concessionable," which would require altering clauses of the national mining code. He has the full support of Chile's mining chamber, but any changes would need to pass through both Congress and Senate, where his allies are a minority.

**Legislative Implication:**
Proposed legislation would eliminate Article 5 of Decree Law 2,886 and modify Article 8° of Law 18,302 (which creates CCHEN). If enacted, this would remove lithium's non-concessionable status and — most importantly for this analysis — potentially modify or eliminate CCHEN's "legal act" authorization requirement. An acquirer who can time a transaction to coincide with this legislative change (if enacted) would face a materially reduced CCHEN risk. However: (a) the legislative timeline is uncertain; (b) transition provisions for existing CORFO contracts are unknown; and (c) CORFO's contractual rights survive any legislative change not specifically addressing existing contract terms.

### 5c. The Albemarle/Codelco Discussion — The Live Negotiation Variable


Albemarle's current contract runs through 2043, and the company is in discussions with Codelco.
 This single sentence — from a late 2025 ICCT research report — is among the most consequential facts for M&A structuring. If Albemarle and Codelco are actively negotiating a future state-participation arrangement, an acquirer of Albemarle could find itself:

1. **Inheriting a near-complete negotiation** that, if closed, would result in Codelco taking a significant ownership position in Albemarle Ltda. before the acquirer even completes its acquisition of Albemarle Corporation.
2. **Disrupting an ongoing negotiation** that CORFO and Codelco view as central to Chile's lithium strategy — triggering political opposition even without formal legal barriers.
3. **Required to recommit to the negotiation framework** as a condition of any CORFO acquiescence to the ownership change.

This ongoing negotiation is a critical pre-acquisition diligence item that must be investigated directly through Albemarle's management and Chilean government channels.

---

## SECTION 6: FDI SCREENING AND APPLICABLE REGULATORY MECHANISMS

### 6a. Chile Has No Formal FDI Screening Mechanism

Chile does not have an investment screening mechanism analogous to CFIUS (U.S.) or FIRB (Australia). FDI formal approval procedures are expeditious, and investments are generally approved without a dedicated national security review process. There is no buyer-nationality restriction in Chile's formal FDI framework.

### 6b. The FNE as the Operative Review Body

Based on the Tianqi/SQM precedent, FNE merger control review is the most clearly established regulatory hurdle for any acquisition that concentrates positions in the global lithium market.


The head of Chile's economic development agency CORFO, Jose Miguel Benavente, said a dozen companies from various countries are interested in obtaining lithium through the Albemarle offering, positioning Chile to take a bigger role in the global supply chain of battery components.


An acquisition of Albemarle Corporation — one of the world's two largest lithium producers — will almost certainly meet FNE notification thresholds. CORFO will likely intervene as an interested party, citing market concentration concerns analogous to those it raised in the Tianqi/SQM proceeding. The conditions imposed (information barriers, governance restrictions, behavioral remedies) could be commercially significant regardless of whether formal consent is required.

### 6c. Buyer-Nationality Risk Calibration

| Buyer Type | FNE Risk | CORFO Opposition | Political Risk | CCHEN Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chinese state-linked (Ganfeng, Tianqi, CATL) | HIGH — market concentration, Tianqi precedent | HIGH — CORFO objected to Tianqi/SQM minority stake | MAXIMUM — cross-party opposition | HIGH |
| U.S. industrial/financial | MEDIUM — depends on lithium market position | LOW-MEDIUM | LOW-MEDIUM (MSP alignment helps) | LOW-MEDIUM |
| Japanese trading house | MEDIUM | LOW | LOW (MSP ally) | LOW |
| Saudi/Gulf sovereign wealth | LOW-MEDIUM (usually financial, not strategic) | LOW | MEDIUM (geopolitical context) | MEDIUM |
| European industrial | LOW-MEDIUM | LOW | LOW (EU-Chile FTA) | LOW |
| Private equity (no mining operations) | LOW | LOW | LOW-MEDIUM | LOW |

**The CORFO Market Concentration Warning:**

If any of the two (Albemarle or Tianqi) grabbed the stake in SQM, it would mean the world's three top producers would own between 70% and 80% of the total market, Eduardo Bitrán, executive vice-president of Corfo, warned. "We don't want that. It will create conflict of interest that will upset the interests of SQM, the interests of Corfo, the interests of Chile and that's a problem."
 This statement — made about a minority SQM stake — applies with even greater force to an acquisition of Albemarle itself.

## SECTION 7: PRACTICAL TRANSACTION STRUCTURING

### 7a. The Structural Toolkit

**(i) Whole-Company Acquisition at U.S. Parent Level — Standard and Most Precedent-Supported Structure**

Based on the historical precedent (Foote → Chemetall → Rockwood → Albemarle) and CORFO's own official language recognizing the corporate succession, an upstream whole-company acquisition is the most commercially standard approach. The historical precedent suggests this structure has not previously triggered formal CORFO consent proceedings. However, the 2016 and 2024 contract amendments may have added provisions not present in the pre-2015 contract, and the political/institutional environment has changed categorically.

**(ii) Structural Separation — Acquiring Albemarle Minus Chilean Assets**
Not commercially viable for a lithium-focused buyer. The Chilean CORFO contract is the core asset.

**(iii) Pre-Transaction CORFO Engagement — The Legally Cleanest Path**
Proactive CORFO engagement prior to signing provides legal certainty at the cost of opening a negotiating window. 
Albemarle noted they are "proud of our more than 40-year relationship with Corfo" and committed to finding "an agreement that benefits both Chile and Albemarle."
 A sophisticated acquirer should anticipate that pre-transaction CORFO engagement will result in some additional commitments — potentially including accelerated royalty terms, enhanced environmental obligations, or commitment to the Codelco negotiation framework. These are price-able costs relative to the acquisition's strategic value.

**(iv) Albemarle/Codelco Pre-Closing Resolution**
An acquirer that completes the Albemarle/Codelco negotiation framework before closing — potentially with Codelco taking a formal stake in Albemarle Ltda. as part of the deal — would eliminate the single largest source of Chilean political opposition. However, this approach requires coordination between the acquirer and Codelco before deal announcement, creating significant information asymmetry and governance complexity.

### 7b. The Timing Optionality — Kast Legislative Reform

If the Kast government successfully advances legislation making lithium concessionable — amending or repealing CCHEN's "legal act" authorization requirement under Law 16,319 — the regulatory landscape changes materially. An acquirer with timeline flexibility should monitor this legislative development closely as a potential transaction timing trigger.

### 7c. Albemarle's 10-K as a First Diligence Step

The fastest, most immediately actionable step is reviewing Albemarle's annual 10-K filings for specific risk factor language about the CORFO contract and change-of-control scenarios. Under SEC disclosure rules, material contract risks must be disclosed. The absence of specific change-of-control risk disclosure language is itself a material data point — but the full EDGAR exhibits (including any filed contract excerpts) should be reviewed before reaching a final conclusion.

## SECTION 8: CONSOLIDATED RISK ASSESSMENT MATRIX

### 8a. Mandatory CORFO Contractual Consent or Notification Requirement

**Probability: LOW-MEDIUM (materially revised downward from prior version)**

*Revised Reasoning:* Three independent pieces of evidence now support a lower probability:
1. CORFO's own official press release for Annex 2024 acknowledges the Foote → Albemarle US Inc. corporate succession as an accepted matter of administrative record — without referencing any consent requirement for that succession.
2. Albemarle's SEC 10-K risk disclosures do not specifically identify CORFO change-of-control consent as a material risk, under disclosure obligations that would require such identification if the risk were material.
3. The October 2024 Rio Tinto/Albemarle M&A speculation attracted zero public response from CORFO, the Chilean Ministry of Mining, or any regulatory body about consent requirements.

*Remaining Uncertainty:* The full contract text — particularly the 2016 Amendment and Annex 2024 — has not been reviewed. If a change-of-control clause exists and was inserted in 2016 (after the Rockwood acquisition), the probability would increase significantly. Contract text review remains the single most important diligence step.

### 8b. CORFO Right or Willingness to Renegotiate Material Terms

**Probability: MEDIUM-HIGH (politically), LOW-MEDIUM (strictly legally without a consent right)**

*Reasoning:* CORFO has used every contract interaction — the 2016 renegotiation, the 2018-2019 arbitration threat, and the 2021-2024 ICC arbitration — as an opportunity to extract improved terms. 
"Lithium is a strategic resource for the future of the country, and it is totally unacceptable that Albemarle does not fulfill the contract when the agreement is less favorable," Corfo's executive vice-president said.
 An acquisition creating a new ultimate beneficial owner would be the most significant change in the contract relationship since 1980 and would provide significant political and potentially legal impetus for CORFO to demand renegotiation — even without a formal consent right. The ongoing Albemarle/Codelco discussions make this risk particularly acute.

### 8c. CORFO Right to Terminate the Exploitation Contract

**Probability: LOW — Conditional on Breach or Explicit Termination Right**

*Reasoning:* Termination would be economically and reputationally catastrophic for Chile. 
The new preferential pricing process follows the resolution of a four-year dispute between CORFO and Albemarle, in which Chile gave Albemarle the ability to increase its lithium production by nearly 50%, contingent on developing new technology aimed at more environmentally friendly extraction.
 CORFO's posture — even during a four-year contentious arbitration — was to renegotiate rather than terminate. Termination risk exists only if: (a) a change-of-control clause exists and is violated without cure; or (b) a buyer so egregiously offends Chilean sovereignty that termination becomes politically inevitable. Even then, Albemarle would likely have a U.S.-Chile FTA investor protection claim that would make termination extraordinarily costly for Chile.

### 8d. CCHEN Authorization Requirement

**Probability: MEDIUM-HIGH that CCHEN authorization is legally required for structural transactions affecting the exploitation right; MEDIUM-LOW that CCHEN would interpret an upstream U.S. parent acquisition as a covered "legal act"**

*Revised Reasoning:* The statutory mandate is clear and broad. The SQM/Codelco precedent confirms CCHEN authorization is required for structural transactions directly affecting CORFO exploitation contract holders. However, CORFO's official acknowledgment of the Foote → Albemarle succession without referencing any CCHEN authorization process suggests CCHEN has historically not interpreted its mandate to cover upstream corporate ownership changes. If CCHEN maintains this interpretation, a whole-company Albemarle acquisition would not require CCHEN authorization. If CCHEN expands its interpretation — consistent with its broader oversight scope since 2016 — it could. Direct CCHEN engagement is the only way to resolve this.

### 8e. FNE Antitrust Review

**Probability: HIGH — Near-Certainty for Buyers with Lithium Market Presence**

*Reasoning:* Based on Tianqi/SQM precedent, FNE review is the most clearly established regulatory requirement. CORFO will intervene as an interested party. 
The price depends on the way each company markets its products: a large part of SQM's volumes are spot sales while Albemarle's are through contracts.
 Any buyer who is an existing lithium producer or lithium-chain participant will face FNE market concentration analysis. The behavioral conditions imposed may be the most commercially significant regulatory constraint.

### 8f. Political Opposition that Could Informally Complicate the Transaction

**Probability: HIGH (Chinese buyer), MEDIUM (U.S./European buyer), MEDIUM-HIGH (any buyer, given Codelco negotiation)**

*Reasoning:* Even under the more market-friendly Kast government, any acquisition of Albemarle — which would transfer beneficial ownership of the world's most valuable private lithium exploitation right to a new foreign entity — will attract political attention. 
The total value of exports of all lithium products in 2023 were primarily exported to China and South Korea, amounting to US$7.8 billion. The government is also expanding mining data collection and hiring technical experts with the aim of increasing the state's leverage to maximize public revenue in mine contract negotiations and strengthening the government's role in developing industrial policy.
 The ongoing Albemarle/Codelco negotiation creates a specific political risk: any acquirer perceived as disrupting or circumventing Chile's state-participation ambitions would face maximum opposition from both the left (who want full nationalization) and the center (who want the Codelco partnership framework).

## NOTABLE ANOMALIES — FINAL VERSION

**ANOMALY 1 — CORFO'S OFFICIAL LANGUAGE IS THE STRONGEST EVIDENCE AGAINST A CONSENT REQUIREMENT**

The single most important anomaly is that CORFO's official press release for Annex 2024 — a government document with legal significance, reviewed by the Comptroller General — explicitly acknowledges the full corporate succession from "Foote Minerals Company, today Albemarle US Inc." without any reference to a prior consent process, CCHEN authorization, or novation requirement for any of the intermediate corporate changes. If CORFO had a mandatory consent right for upstream corporate acquisitions and had exercised it — or had it exercised against it — this official document would be the natural place for CORFO to reference the authorized corporate succession. The absence of any such reference strongly suggests that CORFO has not historically treated upstream corporate ownership changes as triggering its contractual rights.

**ANOMALY 2 — ALBEMARLE'S SEC 10-K DOES NOT SPECIFICALLY DISCLOSE A CHANGE-OF-CONTROL RISK**

Albemarle's most recent forward-looking risk disclosures list general contract, regulatory, and acquisition-divestiture risks — but do not specifically identify CORFO change-of-control consent as a material risk. Under SEC Regulation S-K Item 105, material risks must be specifically disclosed. For a contract representing the majority of Albemarle's strategic value, a material change-of-control clause that could allow CORFO to terminate or renegotiate upon an upstream acquisition would almost certainly require specific disclosure. The absence of such disclosure is the second most probative piece of evidence against a mandatory consent requirement — though not conclusive, since Albemarle's counsel may have concluded the risk exists but is remote or unlikely to be enforced.

**ANOMALY 3 — THE CCHEN "NINE AUTHORIZATIONS SINCE 1980" STATEMENT REMAINS UNRESOLVED**

CCHEN has, since 1980, granted nine authorizations, determining specific conditions for each. If CCHEN has only issued nine structural authorizations over 45 years of lithium exploitation — covering multiple corporate ownership changes, two major CORFO contract renegotiations, and one ICC arbitration settlement — then these authorizations cannot be routine sales-level clearances. The most logical interpretation is that they are framework-level structural authorizations. Whether any of these nine authorizations covered the Foote → Chemetall → Rockwood → Albemarle corporate succession chain — or whether the succession proceeded entirely without CCHEN involvement — is the most important unresolved factual question in the entire analysis. If one of the nine authorizations covered a corporate ownership change, the precedent for CCHEN involvement in an acquisition is established. If none did, the argument for CCHEN's narrow interpretation is significantly strengthened.

**ANOMALY 4 — RIO TINTO CHOSE ARCADIUM OVER ALBEMARLE — MARKET-REVEALED COMPLEXITY PREMIUM**

Rio Tinto was specifically named as a potential Albemarle acquirer in October 2024. Within weeks, Rio Tinto instead announced the acquisition of Arcadium Lithium for $6.7 billion — a company with no Chilean CORFO exploitation contract. Simultaneously, Rio Tinto entered Chile through a new ENAMI partnership for a greenfield project. This sequence suggests Rio Tinto — one of the world's most sophisticated mining M&A participants — specifically assessed the CORFO contract complexity and concluded that a new Chilean partnership without pre-existing contractual constraints was preferable to inheriting Albemarle's CORFO position. This is the most important market-revealed evidence that sophisticated acquirers perceive the CORFO contract as creating meaningful transaction complexity, even if the precise legal risk (consent requirement vs. political risk vs. Codelco negotiation entanglement) is difficult to quantify.

**ANOMALY 5 — THE ALBEMARLE/CODELCO NEGOTIATION IS THE MOST IMPORTANT UNDISCLOSED VARIABLE**

The ICCT report confirms Albemarle is in discussions with Codelco — but provides no details about the stage, substance, or structure of these discussions. Depending on what has been negotiated, an acquirer of Albemarle Corporation could face one of several radically different scenarios:
- A near-complete Codelco partnership that would automatically transfer to the acquirer upon closing
- An early-stage conceptual discussion that the acquirer could accept, reject, or renegotiate
- A politically sensitive negotiation that the acquirer's involvement would publicly complicate
- No formal negotiation at all — merely exploratory conversations about the 2024+ National Lithium Strategy framework

The acquirer who understands the Albemarle/Codelco negotiation status has a decisive information advantage in structuring and pricing a transaction.

**ANOMALY 6 — THE CORFO ASSET PURCHASE OPTION IS A POTENTIALLY DECISIVE LEVERAGE MECHANISM**

CORFO retains an option to purchase brine extraction assets under certain conditions, which survived the 2024 settlement unchanged. This option — which has never been publicly triggered or analyzed — may give CORFO significant leverage over any acquirer that materially changes Albemarle's operational approach, strategic direction, or compliance posture. The trigger conditions for this option are unknown from public sources but must be analyzed as a priority diligence item.

**ANOMALY 7 — THE 2024 ANNEX SIGNING-TO-ANNOUNCEMENT GAP AND WHAT IT MAY CONCEAL**


The CORFO agreement was signed on April 26, although it was only made public on May 15, 2024.
 A three-week gap between signing and public disclosure, in the context of a settlement that required Comptroller General review, may reflect deliberate decisions about which provisions to include in the public announcement. The possibility that Annex 2024 contains ownership-notification or change-of-control provisions — inserted quietly as part of a comprehensive contract reset — cannot be excluded. This is a priority item for the Comptroller General's public records review.

**ANOMALY 8 — THE PREFERENTIAL SUPPLY PROGRAM AS AN INDICATOR OF POLITICAL COMPLEXITY**


Chile initially had a bidding process for preferential prices on Albemarle production in 2018 with three companies, but the deal fell through following problems with supply and prices.
 
The new process follows the resolution in which Chile gave Albemarle the ability to increase its lithium production by nearly 50%. The head of CORFO, Jose Miguel Benavente, said a dozen companies from various countries are interested in obtaining lithium through the Albemarle offering.
 Albemarle's preferential supply obligation — which has twice failed to function as designed — is both a source of political friction and a contractual mechanism that any new owner must manage actively. An acquirer who is also a major lithium buyer (e.g., an OEM or battery manufacturer) may face conflict-of-interest concerns if it is simultaneously the entity required to offer preferential prices to competing downstream manufacturers.

## FINAL DILIGENCE ROADMAP — RANKED BY PRIORITY AND IMMEDIACY

**STEP 1 — IMMEDIATE (Before LOI/Term Sheet) — Albemarle's EDGAR Filings**
Review Albemarle's most recent 10-K annual report and all SEC material contract exhibits for: (a) specific risk factor language about CORFO change-of-control consent; (b) any filed exhibits of contract excerpts or summaries; (c) 10-K disclosures about the CORFO contract under "Government Contracts" or "Material Agreements." This is achievable within 48 hours and will provide the most immediate directional signal about what Albemarle's own counsel believes the contract requires.

**STEP 2 — BEFORE SIGNING — Full CORFO Contract Text and Comptroller General Records**
Obtain the complete text of the CORFO exploitation contract (1980 original, 2016 Amendment, and Annex 2024) including: (a) the Comptroller General's toma de razón resolution for Annex 2024; (b) the Comptroller General's toma de razón for the 2016 Amendment. Both documents are public records accessible through Chile's transparency law framework. Focus specifically on assignment, transfer, change-of-control, novation, and termination provisions. Retain Carey y Cía. or Claro & Cía. for this review.

**STEP 3 — BEFORE SIGNING — CCHEN Direct Engagement**
Commission a formal Chilean law opinion on whether Article 8 of Law 16,319 requires CCHEN authorization for an upstream corporate acquisition that does not change the Chilean operating entity. Simultaneously, initiate informal engagement with CCHEN to understand their current interpretive position. Specifically ask CCHEN what the "nine authorizations since 1980" represent and whether any covered corporate ownership changes.

**STEP 4 — BEFORE SIGNING — Albemarle/Codelco Negotiation Status**
Through management diligence and direct Codelco engagement, determine: (a) what has been discussed between Albemarle and Codelco; (b) whether any term sheet, MOU, or heads of agreement exists; (c) Codelco's and CORFO's expectations about the outcome of these discussions; and (d) whether a new acquirer would be expected to continue, accelerate, or renegotiate these discussions.

**STEP 5 — BEFORE SIGNING — FNE Pre-Notification Assessment**
Engage Chilean competition counsel (Morales & Besa or FerradaNehme) to assess: (a) FNE notification requirements; (b) the likely conditions FNE would impose based on the specific buyer's market position; and (c) the expected timeline for FNE review, which may be the critical path for transaction closing and which may impose behavioral conditions (as in the Tianqi/SQM case) that affect the transaction value.

**STEP 6 — BEFORE SIGNING — CORFO Asset Purchase Option**
Identify and analyze the trigger conditions for CORFO's retained option to purchase brine extraction assets. This provision — which survived the 2024 settlement unchanged — may create ongoing leverage over any acquirer that changes operational direction.

**STEP 7 — BEFORE LOI — Government Relations Assessment**
Assess the political environment under the Kast government through direct engagement with Chilean mining-sector government relations advisors. Determine: (a) the Kast government's posture toward a specific acquirer; (b) the likelihood and timeline of concessionability legislation; and (c) the political price of completing the Albemarle/Codelco negotiation versus disrupting it.

---

*This analysis incorporates three rounds of targeted research and reflects all publicly available information as of March 2026. The full operative text of the CORFO exploitation contract has not been reviewed. This analysis does not constitute legal advice and must be supplemented by instruction to qualified Chilean mining law counsel with direct access to primary sources, including the contract text, CCHEN, CORFO, and the Comptroller General's records.*

l1_outputs.prompt:
ROLE: You are a senior cross-border M&A strategist specializing in Korean industrial conglomerates and their global battery materials supply chain strategies. You have deep knowledge of POSCO Holdings' corporate structure, strategic planning, M&A history, and the broader Korean government-backed push to secure critical mineral supply chains outside of Chinese control. Your task is to produce a comprehensive assessment of POSCO Holdings as the most probable strategic acquirer of Albemarle Corporation or its key assets.

CONTEXT: POSCO Holdings (KRX: 005490) is a South Korean industrial conglomerate historically anchored in steelmaking that has aggressively pivoted toward battery materials. Key intelligence about the POSCO-Albemarle nexus:

(1) POSCO Future M (POSCO's battery materials subsidiary) is a major cathode/anode materials producer that requires large volumes of lithium hydroxide and carbonate as feedstock.

(2) In November 2025, POSCO Holdings signed a binding agreement to acquire 30% of a new entity holding MinRes's 50% interests in both the Wodgina and Mt Marion lithium mines for US$765M, with closing expected H1 2026. This transaction values MinRes's 50% at ~$3.9B. As part of this deal, POSCO will receive spodumene concentrate in proportion to its 30% interest.

(3) Albemarle holds the other 50% of Wodgina through the MARBL JV with MinRes. POSCO is now an indirect 15% stakeholder in Wodgina through MinRes's holding entity.

(4) A $350M customer prepayment for future lithium deliveries was received by Albemarle in January 2025. The customer is unnamed, but POSCO is the most likely candidate given geographic/strategic fit.

(5) South Korea represented ~15.4% of Albemarle's FY2023 revenue (~$800M+), likely split across POSCO, Samsung SDI, and LG Energy Solution.

(6) POSCO has been building cathode production capacity in both Korea and North America (including a JV plant in Canada) that requires IRA-compliant, non-Chinese lithium feedstock.

RESEARCH TASKS:

1. POSCO HOLDINGS FINANCIAL CAPACITY ASSESSMENT
a) Research POSCO Holdings' current market capitalization, enterprise value, net debt, cash position, undrawn credit facilities, and credit ratings (Moody's/S&P/Korean agencies) as of the most recent available date.
b) Calculate POSCO's maximum acquisition capacity: cash + available credit + potential equity issuance (at what dilution?) + potential asset divestitures. Could POSCO financially execute a $22B+ acquisition of Albemarle? What about a $25-30B take-private if required to pay a 15-30% premium?
c) Research POSCO's debt capacity — what leverage ratio could Korean banks and international lenders support for a transformative acquisition? Has POSCO ever executed a deal above $5B? Above $10B?
d) Could POSCO structure a consortium acquisition with Korean sovereign entities (Korea Investment Corporation, Korea Development Bank, Korea Mine Rehabilitation and Mineral Resources Corporation — KOMIR)?

2. POSCO'S BATTERY MATERIALS STRATEGY — FULL MAP
a) Research POSCO Future M's stated strategy, production capacity (current and planned), and raw material sourcing approach. How much lithium does POSCO Future M consume annually, and what is projected consumption by 2028-2030?
b) Map all of POSCO's lithium supply agreements, JVs, and investments globally. Include: the Wodgina/MinRes deal, any offtake agreements with Albemarle, any agreements with SQM, Ganfeng, Pilbara Minerals, or others.
c) Research POSCO's lithium hydroxide conversion capacity — does POSCO have its own conversion plants, or does it rely on toll conversion? The Kemerton asset (idled Albemarle hydroxide plant in Western Australia) is directly relevant: has POSCO or any POSCO affiliate publicly discussed Kemerton?
d) Research POSCO's North American battery materials investments — JVs with GM, Ultium, or other OEMs. Do these require IRA-compliant lithium feedstock? Is Albemarle a named or logical supplier for these JVs?

3. THE BREADCRUMB TRAIL — SEQUENCE OF POSCO MOVES
Construct a detailed chronological timeline of every POSCO action related to lithium, battery materials, and Albemarle specifically, from 2020 to present. Include: investments, JV formations, offtake agreements, executive statements, analyst call commentary, press releases, and any M&A activity. The goal is to identify whether POSCO's moves follow a deliberate vertical integration pathway whose logical next step is an Albemarle acquisition.

4. KOREAN GOVERNMENT AND INDUSTRIAL POLICY CONTEXT
a) Research the Korean government's critical minerals strategy — has the government publicly identified lithium supply security as a national priority? Are there government incentive programs that would support a Korean acquisition of a Western lithium producer?
b) Research the Korea-U.S. relationship on critical minerals — is there a bilateral agreement (similar to the Australia-U.S. critical minerals compact) that would facilitate or complicate a Korean acquisition of Albemarle?
c) Would a POSCO acquisition of Albemarle be viewed favorably by the U.S. government given Korea's "friendly nation" status for IRA purposes? Research any CFIUS precedents for Korean acquisitions of U.S. critical mineral assets.

5. THE $350M PREPAYMENT — POSCO AS COUNTERPARTY
a) Research whether POSCO or any POSCO affiliate has disclosed a lithium prepayment or supply security agreement with Albemarle in its Korean-language filings (DART system), press releases, or investor presentations.
b) If POSCO is confirmed or strongly implicated as the prepayment counterparty, analyze the strategic implications: a $350M prepayment is not a routine supply contract — it is a strategic capital deployment that creates a quasi-equity claim on Albemarle's production. Does this create a blocking mechanism (change-of-control termination right on the prepayment) or an inside track (pre-existing commercial relationship that de-risks a broader acquisition)?
c) What are standard change-of-control provisions in lithium prepayment agreements? Would a competing acquirer need to assume or refinance this obligation?

6. POSCO vs. OTHER KOREAN/JAPANESE BUYERS
a) Compare POSCO's positioning against Samsung SDI, LG Energy Solution, and Panasonic — are any of these companies equally or more motivated to acquire upstream lithium assets?
b) Research whether any Japanese trading houses (Mitsui, Mitsubishi, Sumitomo) have expressed interest in lithium M&A — these entities have historically been the principal corporate acquirers of Australian mining assets.
c) Could a Korean or Japanese consortium form to acquire Albemarle jointly? Research precedents for such multi-party acquisitions of mining companies.

7. SCENARIO ANALYSIS
a) Full Albemarle acquisition by POSCO — strategic rationale, financial feasibility, regulatory pathway, estimated premium required, probability assessment (1-10).
b) POSCO acquisition of Kemerton only — as a vertical integration play from Wodgina spodumene to hydroxide. Estimated price, strategic fit, probability.
c) POSCO acquisition of Albemarle's Wodgina 50% stake directly — would this trigger the MARBL JV's ROFR or consent provisions? Price using the MinRes/POSCO comp.
d) POSCO-led consortium acquisition of Albemarle — with KIC, Korean banks, and potentially an Australian or Chilean sovereign entity. Feasibility assessment.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Structured chronological and analytical sections. Cite POSCO's Korean-language disclosures and DART filings where possible. All financial estimates must show methodology. Flag data gaps.

End your output with "NOTABLE ANOMALIES" — anything surprising. Pay particular attention to:
- Any evidence POSCO has quietly accumulated Albemarle shares (check 13F filings for POSCO affiliates)
- Timing correlations between POSCO moves and Albemarle corporate actions
- Any public denials by POSCO of interest in Albemarle that contradict revealed behavior
- Internal POSCO organizational changes (e.g., creation of a lithium M&A team or hiring of M&A advisors)
- Korean press reports about POSCO's lithium strategy not reflected in English-language sources

## CRITICAL GAP IDENTIFICATION — ROUND 3

Reviewing both prior rounds with discipline, three questions remain **genuinely unresearched** and would materially change probability assessments:

**GAP 1 — NPS (Korean National Pension Service) 13F HOLDINGS OF ALB:** In Round 2, I identified NPS as the most important Korean institutional investor to check — an $800B+ AUM fund that would be the natural vehicle for quiet pre-acquisition ALB accumulation — but never searched for it. KIC sold 87.9% of its ALB. NPS's position (accumulating or reducing?) is the complementary data point that could resolve the "Korean sovereign interest is cooling" vs. "KIC rotated from passive to deal mode" ambiguity.

**GAP 2 — ALBEMARLE'S CHARTER AMENDMENT / SUPERMAJORITY REMOVAL:** I flagged this as the single most important corporate governance signal in Round 2 — a management-sponsored proposal to remove supermajority takeover defenses at the May 5, 2026 AGM — but never searched for the specifics: What exactly is being removed? What do ISS/Glass Lewis say? Has any activist investor disclosed a position and pushed for this? This directly governs the M&A vulnerability timeline.

**GAP 3 — ANY FORMAL ALBEMARLE STRATEGIC REVIEW OR BANKER ENGAGEMENT:** I searched broadly in Round 2 and found nothing. A more targeted search for specific advisor names, activist positions, or board-level strategic review disclosures in ALB's proxy materials could resolve whether management is passively waiting or actively marketing.

These three — and only these three — are searched now.
The three targeted searches have returned highly material new intelligence. Let me now compile the definitive, fully consolidated final report incorporating all three rounds of research.

---

# POSCO HOLDINGS AS STRATEGIC ACQUIRER OF ALBEMARLE CORPORATION
## COMPREHENSIVE M&A ASSESSMENT — DEFINITIVE CONSOLIDATED EDITION
**Classification: Analytical Intelligence Report | Date: March 19, 2026**
**Research Rounds Incorporated: 3 | Total Targeted Searches: ~15**

---

## ROUND 3 CRITICAL GAP UPDATE — WHAT THE NEW SEARCHES REVEALED

### Gap 1 — NPS 13F: THE BOMBSHELL FINDING

This is the most consequential single new data point in the entire three-round analysis. The results reveal a **direct contradiction** between NPS behavior toward KIC behavior:


The National Pension Service **lifted its stake in Albemarle Corporation by 261.9% during the 2nd quarter** of 2025, according to its most recent Form 13F filing with the SEC. The fund owned 1,636 shares of the specialty chemicals company's stock after acquiring an additional 1,184 shares during the quarter. NPS's holdings in Albemarle were worth $103,000 at the end of the most recent reporting period.



Additionally, the National Pension Service raised its stake in Albemarle Corporation by **74.5% in the 1st quarter** of 2025. The institutional investor owned 452 shares of the specialty chemicals company's stock after buying an additional 193 shares during the quarter. NPS's holdings in Albemarle were worth $33,000 at the end of the most recent quarter.


**Critical Interpretation:** The absolute dollar values ($33K and $103K) are trivially small — effectively rounding errors for a fund of NPS's scale. 
NPS is the third largest pension fund in the world with over $900 billion in assets.
 
As of the first half of 2025, the NPF's assets under management stood at approximately $920 billion USD.
 A $103,000 position in ALB is not a strategic investment — it is a passive index rebalancing artifact. **NPS is emphatically NOT quietly accumulating ALB shares.** This definitively closes the "Korean sovereign quiet accumulation" hypothesis for NPS. Combined with KIC's 87.9% reduction (Round 2), the picture is clear: **Korean sovereign wealth entities are not pre-positioning in ALB public equity.** This either means (a) there is no Korean acquisition in progress; or (b) any Korean acquisition architecture will be structured privately, with Korean sovereign entities participating as consortium co-investors in a deal vehicle — not as public market shareholders. Interpretation (b) is structurally consistent with how Korea's sovereign finance entities have participated in past overseas industrial acquisitions.

### Gap 2 — Albemarle Charter Amendment: SPECIFIC PROVISIONS IDENTIFIED


At the 2025 annual meeting of shareholders, a majority of shareholders supported a shareholder proposal regarding simple majority voting. Based on that vote and shareholder engagement feedback, the Board is asking shareholders to approve an amendment to Albemarle's Charter to remove provisions requiring the support of **75% of the votes entitled to be cast** for affiliated transactions. Approval of this amendment would remove the **last supermajority vote standards** from the Company. The Board recommends a vote FOR amendment.


This is more specific and more significant than Round 2 indicated. The 75% supermajority threshold being removed is specifically for **"affiliated transactions"** — which in corporate law typically refers to transactions with large shareholders (10%+ holders) and related parties. This provision, if retained, could have required a 75% shareholder vote to approve a transaction where a large strategic investor (e.g., POSCO, if it held >10% of ALB) proposed to acquire the remaining shares. Its removal makes a **negotiated affiliated-party acquisition structurally easier** to complete.

**The 2025 precursor:** A majority of shareholders already voted in favor of simple majority voting in 2025. The board is now implementing that mandate. This is shareholder-driven governance cleanup — not management initiating a sale process. However, the downstream M&A effect is real regardless of intent.

**Glass Lewis's nuanced 2026 position** on such removals adds complexity: 
Glass Lewis revised its policy to clarify that it will evaluate proposals to abolish supermajority voting requirements on a case-by-case basis. While GL generally supports eliminating supermajority thresholds, in certain instances — such as at companies with large or controlling shareholders — these provisions may serve to protect the interests of minority shareholders. In analyzing these proposals, GL will take into account additional factors including shareholder structure, quorum requirements, **impending transactions** (involving the company or a major shareholder) and any internal conflicts within the company.


The italicized language — "impending transactions involving the company or a major shareholder" — is directly relevant. If Glass Lewis knows of or suspects an impending transaction, it could oppose the supermajority removal on minority-shareholder protection grounds. The fact that Glass Lewis has not publicly opposed this amendment (no evidence found) suggests GL has no information suggesting an impending transaction is underway.

### Gap 3 — Albemarle Strategic Posture: NO M&A PROCESS; STRONG OPERATIONAL MOMENTUM

No evidence of hired M&A advisors, activist campaigns targeting ALB, or a formal strategic review was found. Instead, Albemarle is executing a disciplined standalone strategy with strong financial momentum. Key confirmed 2025 actuals and 2026 indicators:


Albemarle reported Q4 net sales of $1.4 billion, up 16% year-over-year, with double-digit volume growth and adjusted EBITDA of $269 million, up 7%. Full-year 2025 net sales reached $5.1 billion and adjusted EBITDA was $1.1 billion, both at or above previous outlooks, reflecting strong energy storage growth and cost improvements. The company achieved $450 million in run-rate cost and productivity improvements in 2025 and reduced CapEx by 65% year-over-year.



Albemarle generated nearly $700 million in 2025 free cash flow and expects positive full-year free cash flow potential if current lithium prices persist.



In 2025, demand growth (~30% y/y) outpaced supply growth (~20% y/y) leading to tight inventories and increased pricing by year end.



Management hopes that the Foreign Entities of Concern (FEOC) provisions in the Inflation Reduction Act (expanded in the One Big Beautiful Bill) will result in Western lithium prices commanding a premium over Chinese prices. That would strengthen Albemarle's competitive position, given its operations in the U.S. and Chile.


**Two new board appointments (February 26, 2026):** 
Albemarle appointed Michelle T. Collins and Mark R. Widmar to its Board of Directors, effective February 26, 2026. Collins will join the Audit & Finance and Governance & Public Policy committees; Widmar will join Executive Compensation & Talent Development and Safety, Sustainability, Operations & Capital. Collins brings 40+ years in audit and advisory, including senior leadership at Deloitte; Widmar brings CEO and CFO experience at First Solar and other public companies.


**Strategic interpretation of board appointments:** Mark Widmar is the former CEO of First Solar — the dominant US domestic solar panel manufacturer and a company that has benefited enormously from IRA domestic content provisions. His appointment to Albemarle's board is consistent with management's strategy of positioning ALB as the IRA-compliant Western lithium champion. Widmar's expertise in navigating the policy environment for clean energy manufacturing makes him a natural advisor as Albemarle positions for Western lithium price premiums. This is a standalone strategy board appointment, not a transaction advisory appointment.

**Confirmed: No formal Albemarle strategic review or M&A process is underway as of March 19, 2026.** The company is executing confidently as a standalone entity.

---

# COMPLETE DEFINITIVE REPORT — ALL THREE ROUNDS CONSOLIDATED

---

## SECTION 1: POSCO HOLDINGS — FINANCIAL CAPACITY (FINAL)

### 1a. POSCO Full-Year 2025 Financials (Confirmed)

| Metric | KRW (Trillion) | USD (~1,350 KRW/USD) |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue | ₩69.1T | $51.2B |
| Operating Profit | ₩1.83T | $1.36B |
| Net Profit (Attributable) | ₩0.66T | $0.49B |
| Total Assets | ₩105.2T | $77.9B |
